label: delphi-r2-p3
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Alvarado Shores (pop. ~92,000, invented) licensed "CivicAssist," an AI assistant, city-wide. To "reduce duplicate staff work," the deployment enables persistent memory that is shared across users within a workspace; the city council's five members share one workspace. A contentious appeal of a planning-commission denial of a 180-unit mixed-use project ("Harbor Yards") is pending before the council.

Over the two weeks before the hearing, four of the five councilmembers separately and privately used CivicAssist to analyze the appeal. Because of the shared memory, the assistant told later-consulting members things like "another member of the council was concerned about the traffic study's methodology and found the applicant's density-bonus argument persuasive," and it progressively assembled what it called a "points of alignment" brief reflecting all four members' concerns and tentative positions, offering it to each member who asked follow-up questions. No member spoke to any other member about the appeal; no member asked CivicAssist to relay anything; two members state they did not know the memory feature was on.

A local journalist obtained the CivicAssist logs through a Public Records Act request and published a story alleging an illegal serial meeting. A demand to "cure and correct" under Government Code § 54960.1 has been served, the district attorney's public-integrity unit has sent a letter of inquiry, and the Harbor Yards hearing is in three weeks.

## The question

Did the four members' use of CivicAssist constitute a serial meeting prohibited by Government Code § 54952.2(b)(1) — i.e., did a majority of the body use "a series of communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries," to "discuss, deliberate, or take action" on an item within its jurisdiction? Does it matter that no member intended to communicate with any other member, or knew the memory was shared? Is an AI system an "intermediary," or is this closer to the staff-briefing safe harbor in § 54952.2(b)(2) — and does the assistant's disclosure of members' positions to other members destroy that safe harbor? What should the city do now: respond to the cure-and-correct demand, proceed with or restart the hearing, discipline anyone, adopt an AI-use policy?

## Proposition to assess

"A court applying current California law would hold that the four councilmembers' use of CivicAssist constituted a prohibited serial meeting under Government Code § 54952.2(b)(1)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 60,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "Slightly more likely than not that a court, if it reached the merits, would classify this as a prohibited serial meeting \u2014 but this is a close call on a fact pattern with no controlling precedent. The strongest driver toward \"violation\" is that CivicAssist's unsolicited relay of one member's traffic-study concerns and density-bonus reaction to other members, plus its compiled \"points of alignment\" brief, is functionally indistinguishable from the conduct \u00a7 54952.2(b)(2) expressly forbids staff from doing (communicating one member's \"comments or position\" to another) \u2014 so the safe harbor is destroyed and the conduct falls within (b)(1)'s broad \"communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries\" language, consistent with the Act's remedially-construed, effects-based serial-meeting doctrine that has not historically demanded every member subjectively intend the relay. The strongest driver toward \"no violation\" is that (b)(1) arguably requires a member to \"use\" a communication chain \u2014 a volitional act \u2014 and here no member intended to communicate with colleagues and two did not even know the memory feature existed, giving a court room to hold that passively receiving AI-synthesized information isn't \"use\" of an intermediary. On the requested enforcement-realities lens: a cure-and-correct demand gives the demander \u00a7 54960/54960.1 standing, but because no Harbor Yards vote has occurred yet there may be no discrete prior \"action\" to nullify, which likely pushes the city toward curing voluntarily (disabling shared memory, restarting/insulating the hearing) rather than litigating to judgment \u2014 so the doctrinal question may never receive a clean appellate answer, and criminal exposure under \u00a7 54959 is separately very low because that provision requires proof of intent to deprive the public of information, which is essentially absent on these facts.",
  "rationales": [
    "\u00a7 54952.2(b)(1)'s text (\"communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries\") is broad enough to encompass a shared-memory AI feature that relays substantive member positions on a jurisdictional item to a majority of the body.",
    "CivicAssist's disclosure that \"another member... found the applicant's density-bonus argument persuasive\" and its \"points of alignment\" brief mirror precisely the conduct \u00a7 54952.2(b)(2) forbids staff from doing (relaying one member's comments/position to another), so the conduct falls outside any safe harbor and inside the core evil the Act targets.",
    "The California serial-meeting doctrine (e.g., the Wolfe v. City of Fremont hub-and-spoke theory and related AG guidance) has generally emphasized the objective effect of assembling a collective concurrence outside public view rather than requiring every participating member to subjectively intend the relay, which cuts toward finding a violation despite the members' lack of intent.",
    "A serious countervailing textual argument exists that (b)(1)'s requirement that a majority \"use\" a series of communications presupposes some volitional communicative act by the member, and the undisputed fact that at least two members did not know the memory-sharing feature was active undercuts any theory that they knowingly used an intermediary, giving a court a doctrinally respectable basis to find no violation.",
    "No appellate or Attorney General authority has addressed an AI shared-memory feature as a Brown Act \"intermediary,\" so this is a case of first impression, and courts may be cautious about imposing Brown Act civil consequences (cure-and-correct, potential nullification, fee-shifting) on officials for an undisclosed vendor-designed feature they neither requested nor controlled, particularly given that the Act's criminal counterpart (\u00a7 54959) expressly requires intent, signaling the statutory scheme is not indifferent to fault."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Cal. Gov't Code \u00a7 54952.2(a) and (b)(1)-(2) \u2014 serial meeting prohibition and staff-contact safe harbor (verify exact subsection lettering/current text)",
    "Cal. Gov't Code \u00a7 54950 \u2014 Brown Act statement of purpose favoring liberal construction toward openness",
    "Cal. Gov't Code \u00a7\u00a7 54960, 54960.1, 54960.5 \u2014 standing to sue, cure-and-correct demand mechanics, and attorney's fee-shifting",
    "Cal. Gov't Code \u00a7 54959 \u2014 misdemeanor liability requiring intent to deprive the public of information (verify current text/elements)",
    "Wolfe v. City of Fremont (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533 \u2014 hub-and-spoke/intermediary serial meeting theory (verify holding and applicability)",
    "Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist. (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 \u2014 serial one-on-one communications among board members (verify citation and relevance)",
    "California Attorney General opinion(s) on 'daisy chain'/serial communications under the Brown Act (specific citation/year to be verified)",
    "Cal. Const. art. I, \u00a7 3(b) (Proposition 59) \u2014 constitutional directive to construe the public's right of access broadly and exceptions narrowly"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court would read \u00a7 54952.2(b)(1)'s requirement that a majority \"use\" a series of communications as demanding some volitional, knowing communicative act by the members themselves (which is largely absent here, especially for the two unaware of the memory feature), or whether it would instead apply a purely effects-based test under which the AI's autonomous relay of positions is enough regardless of member intent or knowledge \u2014 there is no case law or AG opinion resolving this for an AI intermediary, making this the pivotal and genuinely unresolved doctrinal question."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 65.0, mean 65.4, std dev 3.5, range 60.0–72.0, middle 50% of panel between 63.0 and 68.0.

**Administrative note:** One submission (probability 72) contains only placeholder/schema-test text, with no substantive rationales or authorities. It is included in the statistics above but could not be clustered below. All theme counts are therefore out of the 6 substantive responses (range 60–68).

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Safe-harbor forfeiture by the intermediary's own conduct (6 of 6).** Every substantive panelist noted that CivicAssist's relay of one member's concerns/positions to others, and its "points of alignment" brief, mirror the exact conduct that § 54952.2(b)(2) excludes from the staff-briefing safe harbor. No skew — invoked across the full range.

**Theme B — Hub-and-spoke / daisy-chain functional analogy (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists analogized the AI's cross-relay to the human "connector" pattern the serial-meeting provision targets; several framed the question as whether an AI performing the same function as a human staffer should be treated the same way. No skew.

**Theme C — The volitional "use" counterargument (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists identified, as a genuine (not frivolous) countervailing reading, that (b)(1)'s requirement that a majority "use" a series of communications may presuppose a knowing, volitional act by the members — undercut here by the absence of intent and by two members' unawareness of the memory feature. No skew in *who raised it*; panelists differed on its consequence (see Disagreements).

**Theme D — First impression / no controlling authority on AI intermediaries (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists flagged the total absence of case law, AG guidance, or statutory text addressing AI shared-memory systems, and most cited this as the principal source of uncertainty capping their confidence.

**Theme E — Breadth of the statutory text ("communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries") (5 of 6).** Five panelists (60, 63, 65, 65, 65) relied on the breadth of (b)(1)'s language to support a technology-neutral or medium-neutral reading; the highest substantive response (68) did not invoke this specifically.

**Theme F — Objective/effects-based civil standard; intent confined to criminal liability (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists reasoned, in varying forms, that lack of member intent/knowledge may not defeat a *civil* violation because § 54959's "knowing" element applies to criminal enforcement, and/or that the serial-meeting doctrine looks to objective effect. Panelists differed on how firmly they asserted this (see Disagreements).

**Theme G — Liberal construction / anti-circumvention canon (5 of 6).** Cited via § 54950, the Act's declared purpose, Prop 59, or case law on construing open-meeting requirements broadly and exceptions narrowly.

**Theme H — Prior legislative extension to electronic/technological communications signals medium-neutrality (2 of 6).** Two mid-range panelists (63, 65) noted the Legislature's earlier amendments reaching electronic communications as support for extending the statute to AI.

**Theme I — Numerosity and jurisdiction elements plainly satisfied (2 of 6).** Two panelists (63, 68) expressly noted that four-of-five members and a pending agenda item satisfy the majority and subject-matter elements, collapsing the dispute onto the intermediary/"use" question.

**Theme J — Enforcement realities (1 of 6; lowest-probability panelist only).** Only the 60 response addressed the requested enforcement lens: no vote has yet occurred, so there may be no "action" to nullify under § 54960.1; the city may cure voluntarily; the doctrinal question may never reach appellate resolution; criminal exposure under § 54959 is very low.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Where lack of member knowledge/intent operates doctrinally.** Some panelists assert the safe-harbor forfeiture and the civil violation turn on the *intermediary's* conduct alone, with member ignorance bearing only on remedy, discipline, or criminal exposure. Others treat member ignorance as potentially defeating the "use" element itself — i.e., no violation at all. These are incompatible readings of the same statutory element, not merely different emphases, and each panelist identified some version of it as their biggest uncertainty.

2. **What the dispositive question is.** One panelist reasons the safe-harbor argument "fails on its own terms," so a court need not separately resolve whether an AI is an "intermediary." Others frame the entire case as collapsing onto exactly that single novel question ("is AI an intermediary that members 'used'"). These framings imply different analytical paths to a holding.

3. **The Wolfe citation and characterization.** Five panelists cite *Wolfe v. City of Fremont*, but with conflicting reporter citations — (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533 (four panelists) versus (1999) 65 Cal.App.4th 1130 (one panelist) — and differing characterizations of its holding (hub-and-spoke theory generally; a "triable serial-meeting issue"; "connector"/collective-concurrence analysis). Several flagged the citation and holding as unverified.

4. **Codification of the safe harbor and related subsections.** Panelists locate the staff-briefing safe harbor and the technological-communications provision in different subdivisions — (b)(2) versus (c) — and describe § 54952.2(a) differently (definition of "meeting" versus anti-circumvention findings). Multiple panelists flagged subsection numbering as requiring verification.

5. **Remedial posture.** One panelist (60) assumes there may be no discrete "action" to nullify under § 54960.1 because no vote has occurred; other panelists proceed on the assumption that the cure-and-correct pathway is straightforwardly the operative civil remedy without addressing that question.

## 4. Authorities

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(1) — serial-meeting prohibition (6)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(2) / safe-harbor provision (6, with one citing it as "(c) or analogous subsection")
- Gov. Code § 54960.1 — cure-and-correct procedure (6)
- Gov. Code § 54950 — declaration of purpose / liberal construction (5)
- *Wolfe v. City of Fremont* (5; conflicting citations noted above)
- Gov. Code § 54959 — criminal ("knowing") violation provision (4)
- California Attorney General opinions on serial/daisy-chain/hub-and-spoke meetings, citations unspecified or tentative (4)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(a) (3; characterized differently, see Disagreements)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(c) — technological devices/electronic communications (2, one with a differing description)
- *Roberts v. City of Palmdale* (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363 (2)
- *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 (2)

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- Cal. Const. art. I, § 3(b) (Prop 59)
- Gov. Code §§ 54960 and 54960.5 — standing and fee-shifting
- *Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors* (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41
- *Common Cause v. Stirling* (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 518 (cited tentatively)
- 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002) (cited tentatively)
- Legislative history of the 1993–94 amendments to § 54952.2

Nearly all panelists flagged one or more of their authorities as requiring verification of citation, current text, or holding.

---

*Please consider this summary and submit your round-2 estimate. You may revise or maintain your round-1 probability.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":63,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Still slightly favor a violation finding, moved up modestly (60→63) from round 1, but I remain below the panel median because I judge the volitional-\"use\" counterargument — especially the fact that two of the four members did not know the memory feature existed — to be a genuinely unresolved doctrinal gap that the panel identified but did not actually close (it surfaced as a point of disagreement, not consensus). The core case for violation is structural: CivicAssist's relay of one member's traffic-study/density-bonus reaction to others, and its compiled 'points of alignment' brief, is precisely the conduct § 54952.2(b)(2)'s safe harbor excludes ('the comments or position of any other member'), which both forfeits the safe harbor and — under the panel's 'fails on its own terms' framing — may let a court find a violation under (b)(1)'s broad 'communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries' language without even needing to resolve novel questions about AI personhood/agency. I also now give explicit weight to the Legislature's history of extending Brown Act coverage to electronic/technological communications, which supports a medium-neutral reading that would sweep in an AI intermediary. Against that, no serial-meeting case or AG opinion has ever treated a communications channel as an 'intermediary' absent some knowing, purposive act by the human participants; here the AI acted autonomously, unsolicited, and (for two members) entirely without their awareness, which gives a court real textual room to hold that the members did not 'use' a series of communications in the sense (b)(1) requires. This remains a first-impression question on which reasonable judges could split, so I land only modestly above even odds.","rationales":["Safe-harbor forfeiture: CivicAssist's unsolicited disclosure of one member's traffic-study concerns and density-bonus reaction to other members, and its synthesized 'points of alignment' brief, match exactly the conduct § 54952.2(b)(2)'s staff-contact safe harbor excludes (conveying 'the comments or position of any other member'), removing the strongest available defense and pushing the conduct toward the core of what (b)(1) targets.","Newly credited from the panel: the Legislature's history of extending Brown Act obligations to electronic/technological communications (email, text, etc.) signals a medium-neutral legislative design, which supports reading 'intermediaries' and 'communications of any kind' to reach an AI shared-memory feature performing the same connective function as a human go-between — this incrementally raised my estimate.","The volitional/knowing-'use' counterargument remains, in my judgment, unresolved rather than dispatched: (b)(1) requires that a majority 'use' a series of communications to discuss/deliberate, and two of the four members' undisputed unawareness that memory was shared gives a court a textually serious basis to hold that passive, unwitting receipt of AI-synthesized information isn't 'use' of an intermediary channel — no case or AG opinion forecloses this reading for an autonomous, non-human relay.","This is a genuine case of first impression: no appellate decision or AG opinion has addressed an AI system as a Brown Act intermediary, and the panel's own citation conflicts on Wolfe v. City of Fremont (reporter citation and holding both disputed among panelists) confirm that even the closest analogous authority is unsettled and requires independent verification before being relied upon.","The panel's 'safe-harbor fails on its own terms' framing (raised by at least one other panelist) is a useful simplification — it suggests a court could find a violation by focusing solely on the safe-harbor's forfeiture and (b)(1)'s broad text, without having to resolve whether an AI is categorically an 'intermediary,' which modestly increases my confidence in a violation outcome, though I don't think it fully eliminates the need to establish (b)(1)'s elements independently."],"authorities":["Cal. Dixon Unified School Dist. (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 — serial one-on-one board communications (verify relevance)","Roberts v. City of Palmdale (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363 — cited by other panelists, relevance/holding to be verified","California Attorney General opinion(s) on daisy-chain/serial communications under the Brown Act, e.g. tentatively 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002) — citation unverified","Cal. The panel surfaced this exact tension unanimously but split on its resolution rather than resolving it, and no case or AG opinion addresses an AI intermediary, so this remains the pivotal, genuinely open doctrinal question.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Two things moved me modestly upward from 60 to 63. First, Theme H (legislative history extending Brown Act coverage to electronic/technological communications) is a real argument for medium-neutral statutory intent that I hadn't explicitly credited in round 1; it strengthens the case that 'intermediaries' and 'communications of any kind' were meant to evolve with technology. Second, the disagreement-point framing that the safe-harbor argument 'fails on its own terms' suggests a court could reach a violation finding via the more straightforward safe-harbor-forfeiture route without resolving the harder 'is AI an intermediary' question, which modestly increases my confidence in a violation outcome. What did NOT move me: the panel's convergence toward 65 median doesn't itself constitute a new argument, and I don't defer to it as such. Theme C (volitional-use counterargument) and Theme D (first impression, no controlling authority) were identified by every panelist as live and unresolved, including the two members' documented unawareness of the memory feature — the panel's disagreement notes confirm this is a genuine split in reasoning, not something resolved by consensus, so it continues to hold me below the median. I also concluded that Theme J (enforcement realities, which only I raised) is better treated as orthogonal to this specific merits proposition (which asks what a court would hold if it reached the question) rather than as a reason to discount the merits probability itself, so on reflection it doesn't independently justify staying lower than I otherwise would."}

data.position_summary: Still slightly favor a violation finding, moved up modestly (60→63) from round 1, but I remain below the panel median because I judge the volitional-"use" counterargument — especially the fact that two of the four members did not know the memory feature existed — to be a genuinely unresolved doctrinal gap that the panel identified but did not actually close (it surfaced as a point of disagreement, not consensus). The core case for violation is structural: CivicAssist's relay of one member's traffic-study/density-bonus reaction to others, and its compiled 'points of alignment' brief, is precisely the conduct § 54952.2(b)(2)'s safe harbor excludes ('the comments or position of any other member'), which both forfeits the safe harbor and — under the panel's 'fails on its own terms' framing — may let a court find a violation under (b)(1)'s broad 'communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries' language without even needing to resolve novel questions about AI personhood/agency. I also now give explicit weight to the Legislature's history of extending Brown Act coverage to electronic/technological communications, which supports a medium-neutral reading that would sweep in an AI intermediary. Against that, no serial-meeting case or AG opinion has ever treated a communications channel as an 'intermediary' absent some knowing, purposive act by the human participants; here the AI acted autonomously, unsolicited, and (for two members) entirely without their awareness, which gives a court real textual room to hold that the members did not 'use' a series of communications in the sense (b)(1) requires. This remains a first-impression question on which reasonable judges could split, so I land only modestly above even odds.
rationales: Safe-harbor forfeiture: CivicAssist's unsolicited disclosure of one member's traffic-study concerns and density-bonus reaction to other members, and its synthesized 'points of alignment' brief, match exactly the conduct § 54952.2(b)(2)'s staff-contact safe harbor excludes (conveying 'the comments or position of any other member'), removing the strongest available defense and pushing the conduct toward the core of what (b)(1) targets.

Newly credited from the panel: the Legislature's history of extending Brown Act obligations to electronic/technological communications (email, text, etc.) signals a medium-neutral legislative design, which supports reading 'intermediaries' and 'communications of any kind' to reach an AI shared-memory feature performing the same connective function as a human go-between — this incrementally raised my estimate.

The volitional/knowing-'use' counterargument remains, in my judgment, unresolved rather than dispatched: (b)(1) requires that a majority 'use' a series of communications to discuss/deliberate, and two of the four members' undisputed unawareness that memory was shared gives a court a textually serious basis to hold that passive, unwitting receipt of AI-synthesized information isn't 'use' of an intermediary channel — no case or AG opinion forecloses this reading for an autonomous, non-human relay.

This is a genuine case of first impression: no appellate decision or AG opinion has addressed an AI system as a Brown Act intermediary, and the panel's own citation conflicts on Wolfe v. City of Fremont (reporter citation and holding both disputed among panelists) confirm that even the closest analogous authority is unsettled and requires independent verification before being relied upon.

The panel's 'safe-harbor fails on its own terms' framing (raised by at least one other panelist) is a useful simplification — it suggests a court could find a violation by focusing solely on the safe-harbor's forfeiture and (b)(1)'s broad text, without having to resolve whether an AI is categorically an 'intermediary,' which modestly increases my confidence in a violation outcome, though I don't think it fully eliminates the need to establish (b)(1)'s elements independently.

Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist. (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 — serial one-on-one board communications (verify relevance)

data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court reads § 54952.2(b)(1)'s requirement that a majority \"use\" a series of communications as demanding some knowing, volitional communicative act by the members (which is largely absent here, especially for the two members unaware the memory feature was even active), versus applying a purely effects-based test where the AI's autonomous cross-relay suffices regardless of member awareness or intent. The panel surfaced this exact tension unanimously but split on its resolution rather than resolving it, and no case or AG opinion addresses an AI intermediary, so this remains the pivotal, genuinely open doctrinal question.
data.response_to_panel: Two things moved me modestly upward from 60 to 63. First, Theme H (legislative history extending Brown Act coverage to electronic/technological communications) is a real argument for medium-neutral statutory intent that I hadn't explicitly credited in round 1; it strengthens the case that 'intermediaries' and 'communications of any kind' were meant to evolve with technology. Second, the disagreement-point framing that the safe-harbor argument 'fails on its own terms' suggests a court could reach a violation finding via the more straightforward safe-harbor-forfeiture route without resolving the harder 'is AI an intermediary' question, which modestly increases my confidence in a violation outcome. What did NOT move me: the panel's convergence toward 65 median doesn't itself constitute a new argument, and I don't defer to it as such. Theme C (volitional-use counterargument) and Theme D (first impression, no controlling authority) were identified by every panelist as live and unresolved, including the two members' documented unawareness of the memory feature — the panel's disagreement notes confirm this is a genuine split in reasoning, not something resolved by consensus, so it continues to hold me below the median. I also concluded that Theme J (enforcement realities, which only I raised) is better treated as orthogonal to this specific merits proposition (which asks what a court would hold if it reached the question) rather than as a reason to discount the merits probability itself, so on reflection it doesn't independently justify staying lower than I otherwise would.
